text: The surgeon identifies the ureter and mobilizes the colon from the lateral abdominal wall. The ureter is used as a landmark to trace the hilum of the left-sided non-functioning moiety.

The surgeon meticulously dissects and mobilizes the colon near the hilum of the left-sided non-functioning moiety. The upper pole of the non-functioning moiety is reached, followed by dissection of the lower pole. An aberrant vessel at the lower pole is clipped.

The surgeon identifies the lower pole artery, ureter, and lower pole of the kidney, which are clearly demarcated. Further medial dissection is performed at the height of the left-sided non-functioning moiety, and the left-sided renal vein is identified and looped.

The surgeon carefully dissects the renal artery located behind the renal vein and clips the vein. After controlling the vessels, the dissection continues to free the upper pole of the kidney, revealing the psoas muscle posteriorly.

The surgeon mobilizes the kidney, dismembering the non-functioning moiety from the normal kidney. The collecting system of the normal kidney is opened, and a stay suture is placed at the corner of the incision to provide traction for continued dismemberment. The non-functioning moiety is completely separated from the normal kidney.
The surgeon cuts the ureter and performs a pyelography on the normal kidney. Continuous sutures are applied, and the specimen is placed in a bag for removal. The post-operative specimen is visible.
